segments.text: So now we come to the second book, which is the chapter of the Force, of the Four
Things.
The first two ties four point one, four and three.
The Buddha said, monks possessed of four qualities, the foolish, sinful, inauthentic
man carries about with him and uprooted, lifeless self is blameworthy, is ensured by the
intelligent, and begots, much de-merit, what for?
Without investigation or scrutiny, he speaks in praise of what deserves not praise.
Likewise, he speaks in the praise of that deserving praise.
Without investigation or scrutiny, he shows appreciation where there should be none.
Likewise, when appreciation should be shown, he shows this pleasure, these are the
four qualities.
But monks possessed of four qualities, the wise virtuous, authentic man carries about
with him, a self not uprooted.
Not lifeless, is not blameworthy, is not censured by the intelligent, and begots, much
de-merit, and begots, much merit, what for?
After investigation or scrutiny, he speaks in praise of that deserving praise, likewise,
he speaks in this praise of that deserving praise.
After investigation or scrutiny, he shows appreciation for that deserving appreciation,
likewise, he shows this pleasure for that deserving this pleasure, these are the four.
So here the Buddha is saying that when we praise or we speak ill of anybody or anything,
we should be careful, we should investigate and scrutinize before we make some comment
like that, especially if it is about certain people, especially if they are virtuous and
we speak ill, and that would create a lot of de-merit for us, this way, we have to be very careful.
In the Buddha said, in one of the sutras, whenever we are about to do anything with intention,
before demiscrating command, before we do it, we must be careful, and ask ourselves whether
that action is going to lead to the harm of ourselves or to others, if it is going to lead
to the harm of the self or others, we should not do it, it is going to lead to the benefit
of the self or others, then we can do it.
So we have to consider before we act and then during our action itself, again we should
consider and even after our actions, again we should consider reflected on whatever actions
we have done, whether it created any harm, any suffering to anybody else or to also, or whether
it benefited.
So we have to be careful about our actions.
A 4.1.4, the Buddha said, monks, by wrong conduct towards 4 persons, the foolish, sinful,
inauthentic man carries about with him and uprooted lifeless self, is blameworthy, is censured
by the intelligent and he gets much de-merit, who are the 4 monks, it is by wrong conduct
towards mother, father, potato-tagata, and potato-tagata's disciples, that the foolish, sinful,
inauthentic man, gets much de-merit, but monks, by right conduct towards the same 4 persons,
the wise virtuous, authentic man carries about with him, a self not uprooted, not lifeless,
he is not blameworthy, he gets much merit.
To tell refers to 4 persons, sometimes they are called the 4 fields of merit, and also the
4 fields of de-merit, that is our parents, and then the Buddha himself, and then the Buddha's
disciples, that means monks, and nuns, he see the Buddha is already not in the world,
so we can forget about conduct towards the Buddha, but our parents, especially for a lot
of people, sometimes if we need to collect our parents, or we are unfelial, we don't
do our duty towards our parents, and after they pass away, then we feel a lot of remorse,
and it gives us a lot of pain, so if we provide for our parents and look after them well,
and after they pass away, we bring us a lot of joy.
So to what the Buddha's disciples, monks and nuns, also we have to be careful, especially
if we intend to speak anything evil about monks and nuns, we have to consider very carefully,
and so these are the 4 fields of merit, and the 4 fields of de-merit, also.
Now the Buddha, the next two times 4.1.7, Buddha said, monks, these 4 who are accomplished
in wisdom, disciplined, confident, deeply learned, damma bearers who live according to damma,
eliminate the order, Sangha, which for a monk who is accomplished in wisdom, disciplined,
confident, deeply learned damma bearer who lives according to damma, eliminates the order,
or Sangha, and nun who is accomplished in wisdom, disciplined, confident, etc. Illuminates the order,
so also a male, lazy disciple who is accomplished in wisdom, disciplined, confident,
deeply learned, etc. And the 4th one is a female, lady disciple who is accomplished in
wisdom, discipline, confident, deeply learned, etc. These are the 4 who being accomplished
in wisdom, disciplined, confident, deeply learned damma bearers, living according to damma,
illuminate the order, this is one shootah, where we find that the Sangha refers not only to monks
only to monks and nuns, but also to male disciples and female disciples.
Sometimes it's called the fourfold assembly.
So for example, when we take refuge in the Sangha, sometimes when we take refuge with the Sangha,
we find that it refers to the Bikhus Sangha.
As you heard, some of the earliest utas, where the lay people in the presence of the Buddha,
they say they take refuge in the Buddha, the Dhamma and the Bikhus Sangha.
And sometimes the Sangha refers to this fourfold assembly.
And it's also explained as the eight areas, eight types of areas.
Utas 4.2.13, the Buddha said monks, there are these four ripe efforts.
But for here in a monk generates desire for the non-arizing of evil and profitable states
that have not yet a reason.
He makes an effort sets going energy.
He lays all of and exerts his mind to this end.
He generates desire for the abandoning of evil and profitable states that have a reason.
He makes an effort sets going energy etc.
And then the third one, he generates desire for the arising of profitable states,
not yet a reason.
He makes an effort sets going energy etc.
And the fourth one, he generates desire for the persisting for the non-confusion,
for the cultivation, for the increase, development and augmentation of profitable states
that have a reason.
He makes an effort sets going energy.
He lays all of and exerts his mind to this end.
These monks are the four ripe efforts.
This is what I was talking about.
Samapadana, the four ripe efforts, which is one of the factors of the Aryan eightfold path.
And the first one is to not allow evil and profitable states that have not yet a reason to arise.
This unprofitable, the word is a translation of Akusala, Akusala is unprofitable
or unskilled full states.
Samapadana, sometimes you can see thoughts or so on.
And the thing that comes up in the mind that is unprofitable, evil and skillful,
that leads to suffering leads to remorse.
If there are not a reason, we should not allow it to arise.
Second one, if it is already a reason, un-evil and profitable states that have already a reason,
then we should abandon it.
That's why we have to always observe our mind.
And the third one is if profitable states have not a reason in our mind,
then we generate these profitable states in our mind.
Like for example, energy, intelligence,lessness, the desire to practice spiritual teachings.
And the fourth one, if the profitable states have already a reason, then we should develop it and augment it and cultivate it.
It develops even further.
These are the four right efforts.
Samapadana.
And the next one, as a little to do with it, is 4.2.14.
There are these four efforts, what for?
The effort to restrain the effort to abandon the effort to generate and the effort to nurture.
And of what sort of monks is the effort to restrain.
Here in a monk's ear and object with the eye, does not grasp that it's general features or it's details.
In as much as coveting and dejection evil and profitable states,
might flow in upon one who dwells with his eye, faculty, unrestrained.
He applies himself to such restraint, sets a guard over the eye, faculty, wins the restraint thereof.
Hearing a sound with the ear, or smelling an order with a nose, or tasting a flavour with a tongue, or with body contacting tangibles,
or with mind-cognizing mental states.
He does not grasp at the general features or at the details.
In as much as coveting and dejection evil states, might flow in upon one who dwells with his faculties unrestrained.
He applies himself to such restraint, wins the restraint thereof.
His monks is called the effort to restrain.
I just stopped here from moment to explain.
This passage is about a practitioner in certain places called in Oviya Sangvara, sensory strain.
Certain places is also called inriyasu-gutadwara guarding the doors of the senses.
Because if we practice the spiritual path, and especially if we are interested in meditation,
we should not be concerned so much with the external world.
The Buddha said, always, that we should dwell on our mind, and on our body, the nature of our body,
and on our mind, and the four stations of satipatana also basically is the body and mind.
If we allow our attention to go out into the objects of seeing objects of the eye or objects of sight and objects of hearing,
that means sounds or smells or tastes touch and touch.
Buddha said, these are maras play ground, satin play ground.
If we go out into these six senses doors, we are likely to be caught by maras.
The Buddha said, because if you see anything, and if you are attracted by it, then feelings arise and perception etc.
And then you think about it, and then the chances of evil and profitable states arising are great.
That's why we are interested in meditation. We try not to be this species.
This species does not grasp the word gahi agaha.
It can mean grasp or sees a hold on to. It can also mean take up or accept.
So, we don't see the general features and the nemitta or the details of it.
And I go a bit faster, and then the Buddha sees.
And of what sort monks is the effort to abandon.
Here in the monk does not admit sensual thought there has a reason, but at abundance it expels it, makes an end of it, tries it out of the new existence.
So also we regard to menline and cruel thought there has a reason. He does not admit evil and profitable states arise from time to time.
He drives them out of renewed existence. This monk is called the effort to abandon.
Now what sort is the abandon? Is the effort to generate or create?
Here in the monk generates the factor of enlightenment that is mindfulness or recollection, sati.
That is based on seclusion or dispassion on cessation that ends in self-surrender.
He generates the factor of enlightenment. There is investigation of Dharma.
The factor of enlightenment that is energy. He generates the factor of enlightenment that is delight pity.
There is tranquility that is concentration that is equanimity based on seclusion on dispassion on cessation that ends in self-surrender.
This monk is called the effort to generate or create.
And of what sort monks is the effort to nurture or develop?

Here in the monk natures the favorable concentration sign, the perception of the skeleton, the perception of the worm eaten cups, of the discolored cups, of the featured cups, the perception of the inflated cups.
This is called the effort to nurture these and monks are the four efforts.
So here these four efforts, the first one, the effort to restrain.
He also has got to do with the earliest uta, the screening of non-orising of evil and profitable states.
The second one, the effort to abandon has also got to do with abandoning evil states.
The effort to generate or create means profitable states.
If they have not come up, we generate them and create them.
The fourth one, the choreo-development of profitable states.
Therefore the reason we develop them further, that means the sign of the meditation, sign of concentration, the perception of the skeleton, the corpse, etc.
So these two sutras are related.
The book of the fourth, or the chapter of the fourth, and the Buddha said,
monks, there are these four chief types of beings.
What for?
Chief of those who have personality is Rahul, Lord of the Asuras.
Chief of those who are given to the pleasures of sense is Mandata the Raja.
Chief of those who have Lordship is Marah the wicked one.
In the world of daivas, marahs and brahmas, together with recluses and brahmanas, daivas and mankind,
Atatagata is reckoned chief and Arahan, a perfectly enlightened one.
These monks are the four chief types of beings.
This uta, the first person that the Buddha mentioned is Rahul.
Rahul is the one of the chiefs among the Asuras.
Daivas, heavenly beings, and Rahul is supposed to be very huge, extremely huge.
Sometimes you can see certain drawings of Rahul are solving the moon,
or trying to solve the moon.
So that is Rahul.
And then the second one is Mandata, the Raja.
But this is only the only place where we find the mention of this name.
So we do not know much about this person.
And the third one is Marah, Marah, to a lot of people.
You see it, and Marah is a person who has a lot of power.
And he wheels a lot of power over the minds of beings,
especially those that are lower than his realm.
Marah is supposed to live in the sixth heaven above the human realm.
Every world system you have three realms.
The lowest is the desire of being differentiated into male and female.
And in the desire realm, last, central desire controls all beings.
And in the desire realm, the highest heaven is Satan, he stays in the highest heaven.
But he is not the chief there.
There are other chiefs, he is one of the chiefs there.
And then the second realm and every world system is the form realm.
Where beings have heavenly beings and we very find form.
In fact, because they have such good control over the minds,
and they are supposed to have meditated and attained concentration, strong concentration.
Then only they can be reborn there.
They are means they have attained the jarnas, but we call the jarnas.
So those beings in the form realm, they give up a lot of light.
And because the mind is so strong and they can change themselves into form with the body.
So like for example, Mahabharata sometimes he goes to visit Sakha Devaraja.
The second heaven above us.
And when he comes, they can see him like a ball of light coming from a distance very fast.
And then he stand up in respect.
And then he arrives there.
He changes into a form of like them with a human form.
You know, not me appears like a young boy.
And then he speaks to them.
And the third realm is the formless realm.
The beings are supposed to be formless.
Or sometimes it is interpreted as that form is so fine that they are almost like formless.
And Mahabharata is supposed to be so powerful.
That there is a suta where the Buddha went to visit the Brahma's Brahma's high beings.
Very high heavenly beings.
And the Buddha is trying to teach them.
But Mahabharata was trying to control the minds of some of them.
So that they would not listen to the Buddha.
That's how powerful Mahabharata is.
And the fourth one is the Buddha and Arahan.
There is a city that of all the beings.
The Buddha is trying to teach for all beings.
Because he has gone beyond all beings.
And later we look at other sutras now.
You understand that.
And the next two times four point three point twenty two.
On a certain occasion, monks, I myself was staying at Uruvaila on the bank of the river Naranjara,
under the Goat Herz Banyan.
This after I had become perfectly enlightened.
Then monks, a great number of Brahmins, broken down, old men, aged, far gone in years.
Who had reached, lives and came to visit me where I was.
On reaching me, they greeted me cutiously.
And after the exchange of greetings and courtesy sat down at one side.
As they thus sat, monks, those Brahmins sat this to me.
We have heard it said, master Goatama, that Goatama, the reclusive space, no respect to,
does not rise up in the presence of, does not offer a seat.
To Brahmins, who are broken down, old men, aged, far gone in years, who have reached, lives and.
In as much master Goatama, as the worthy Goatama, does none of these things.
It is not the proper thing to do master Goatama.
Then monks, I thought to myself, in truth, these rebel ones understand, not either the elder or the things which make an elder.
Do a man be old monks, 80 or 90 or 100 years of age.
Then if he be one who speaks out of due season, who speaks things untrue and unprofitable,
things contrary to Dhamma, doctrine, and contrary to Vinaya, discipline.
If he be one who attests, words unworthy to be treasured in the heart, words unseasonable, and devoid of reason, words undiscriminating and not concerned with welfare,
then that one is recon just a foolish elder.
Though a man be young monks, a youth, a mere lad, black head and blessed with his lucky prime, one in the first flush of life,
if he be one who speaks in due season, who speaks things true and profitable, things according to Dhamma and Vinaya,
if he be one who attests, words worthy to be treasured in the heart, words unable, reasonable, discriminating and concerned with welfare,
then that one is reconned a wise elder.
Now, monks, there are these four things which make the elder.
What for?
Herein, among is virtuous, perfect in the patemoca, a precepts,
restrained with the restrained of the patemoca, perfect in the practice of right behavior,
seen danger in the slightest faults.
He undertakes and trains himself in the training of the precepts.
He is learned, is replete with learning, is a heart of learning.
Those doctrines wish lovely at the beginning, lovely in the middle, lovely at the end.
Both in the meaning and the letter of them, which preach the utterly fulfilled, the perfectly purified way of the holy life.
Such doctrines are much heard by him, born in mind, repeated aloud, pondered over, and well penetrated by his vision.
The four stages of Jhana, sometimes translated as mental absorption, I prefer, like states of mentally in condescense.
The four stages of Jhana, which are of the clear mind, which are concerned with the happy life in this very world,
these he wins easily without effort.
By the destruction of the Asavas in this very life,
thoroughly understanding the release by mind, the release by wisdom.
He realizes it, attains it and dwells there in these monks at the four things which make the elder.
And this would turn, we find the Buddha is trying to define what is an elder.
What is an elder is one, who is worthy of respect, considered worthy of respect.
And so the Buddha said, it's not, it's not because a man is old, that is worthy of respect.
If he speaks words, which are not profitable, which are not valuable, which are untrue, etc.
And he's just a foolish old man, but the Buddha said, four things make an elder, four things make a man worthy of respect.
The first one is virtue, that monk is virtuous, that's the first one.
And the second one, he has much learning, that's very much learning.
That means learning of the Dhamma, that he sings.
And the third one, he has attained the four Jhana's four stages of concentration.
And the last one, he has destroyed the Asavas that means he has gone out of the cycle of rebirth, he has become an Arahan.
So these are the four things. The first one, you find sea lions always very important.
Sea lion is moral conduct.
And it is the foundation of the holy life of the spiritual path of a Buddhist man, moral conduct without moral conduct and you can progress.
And there are some foolish people who think they want to practice only wisdom.
And they think all the other things like keeping the precepts, doing charity, learning the Dhamma, not important.
But you cannot make any progress without all these supporting conditions.
For example, the Mangala Sutta is very good Sutta.
And I mentioned all the different ways of attaining blessings.
I see lies, one of them, Dhamma is another.
Much learning, but who such eyes, another, visiting monks who know the Dhamma to discuss the Dhamma,
and studying the Dhamma and discussion of Dhamma, etc. All these foundations of the spiritual path with aid as to progress.
And you see the second factor mentioned here is much learning.
Much learning. The Buddha's disciples are called Sabhaka. Sabhaka means here as a listener.
And they listen to the Dhamma. All of them, that's why listening to the Dhamma is extremely important.
There's a lot of people. They try to practice the holy life without this important foundation.
And they may practice for 20-30 years, but they don't make much progress.
And people see that it's obvious to other people.
But it's not obvious to them. They have a big temple.
Very egoistic, etc.
So, much learning of the Dhamma is very important.
That's why you find the Buddha's disciples that were enlightened.
Some of them are much faster than the Buddha. They came, listened to the Dhamma,
and was taught by the Buddha in a very short while they were enlightened,
compared to the Buddha who took six years of hard work to become enlightened.
And the fourth one is the four Janas.
The four Janas, if a person that gains the Janas, he's worthy of respect.
That's why the Buddha sits in some other sutras.
They give a monk, can attain the Janas even for a short while.
Then the food that he eats, which are given by other people.
He's worthy of the food that he takes, etc.
And also he follows the Buddha's advice. He listens to the Buddha's advice.
The last one, of course, is the highest criteria.
Now, he has destroyed the Asavasva. He has no more ego.
He has destroyed the ego completely.
That is a person who sees all beings.
He sees one with all beings, and he can never harm other beings.
So, those are the qualities that which make a person and elder worthy of respect.
And thanks to Thais 4.3.24.
On a certain occasion, the Exalted One was staying at Sakita in Kalakaspa.
Then the Exalted One addressed the monk saying,
monks, yes Lord, replied those monks to the Exalted One.
The Exalted One said, monks, whatsoever in the world with his divas, etc.
With his horse of recluses and brahmanas of divas and mankind.
whatsoever is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, attained,
searched into pondered, over the by the mind.
All that do I know.
whatsoever is seen, sensed, cognized, attained, searched into pondered, over by the mind.
That have I fully comprehended.
All that is understood by the Tataragata.
But the Tataragata is not subject to it.
If I were to say, I know whatsoever in the world is seen, heard, and so forth.
It would be a falsehood in me.
If I were to say, I both know it and know it not, it would be a falsehood in me.
If I were to say, I neither know it nor am ignorant of it.
It would be a falsehood in me.
That would be a false in me.
That's monks.
Tataragata is a seeer of one is to be seen.
But he has no concede of one is seen.
He has no concede of one has not been seen.
He has no concede of one is to be seen.
He has no concede about the seeer.
Hearing one is to be heard.
He has no concede of one has been heard.
And heard, or not heard, or is to be heard.
He has no concede about the heer.
So also sensing one is to be sensed, cognizing the cognizable.
He has no concede of the thing cognized, or to be cognized, of him who has cognition.
Thus monks, the Tataragata being such a one in things seen, heard sensed, cognized is such.
Moreover, then he who is such, there is none other greater or more excellent, I declare.
This is the end of the sutra.
This is one of the sutras, which is a bit deep and is not easily understood.
Because the state of Buddha, of an enlightened being, we cannot understand.
But basically what Buddha is trying to say is,
even though he is a seeer of one is to be seen.
He has no concede of one is seen.
He has no concede of one has not been seen.
He has no concede of one is to be seen.
He has no concede about the seeer.
So basically here, what he is trying to say is, there is no self-love.
Even though there is seen, there is just the seeer.
There is no I see.
There is no that he go behind.
So he has destroyed the ego.
So he looks at everything a bit different from us.
We have still got the Asavas, the mental outflows.
Whenever we see something through our senses,
we cognize through our senses at the moment.
Our consciousness arises immediately, it is followed by feeling.
Whether it is a pleasant feeling or unpleasant feeling or a neutral one.
And after that there is perception, there is a perception and an idea about that thing.
Like if you hear a sound and then you perceive this, this destroys these repulsive words.
And then after that, the mind starts working.
You start thinking and then anger rises.
And then volition will follow.
He decides what to do or what not to do.
And then this will repeat the process.
And keep on thinking about it.
And this results in Papantya.
It is a proliferation of thoughts.
One thought becomes ten, ten becomes a hundred, etc.
But with the Buddha, no thoughts arise as destroyed the Asavas.
Whatever you see and you just see it the way it is very naturally.
Without eyes, without any proliferation of thoughts.
That is the state of an other hand.
It is quite difficult for us to understand.
It is basically that that whatever happens and there is no eye.
Because the eye arises with the thinking.
That is why thinking is the support system of our ego.
That is why in meditation we try to stop all that thinking.
And you can't stop the thinking until you have still your mind.
The mind is still that all the thoughts have stopped.
Then you are used to that state.
And slowly the mind declines towards that state.
And then otherwise we are used to thinking.
We always live in the world of the six senses.
Very hard to withdraw from the world of the six senses.
